title: OpenAI Fixes 18-Year-Old GNU libunwind Bug by Treating Crash Debugging Like Epidemiology
gist: OpenAI engineers traced mysterious ChatGPT-search crashes to two unrelated bugs: a silently-miscomputing CPU on one Azure host, and an 18-year-old race condition in GNU libunwind's exception-unwinding path. The breakthrough came from 'epidemiological debugging'—analyzing every core dump from the past year for population-level patterns rather than reasoning about individual crashes.

insights.claim: Rockset, a C++ data-infrastructure service, powers ChatGPT's search and data plugins, and its crashes were disrupting that plane.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Reveals a concrete dependency in OpenAI's stack—Rockset underpins ChatGPT retrieval—useful context on the infra behind frontier AI products.
insights.quote: Rockset, the C++ data infrastructure service that powers ChatGPT's search and data plugins

insights.claim: Building a pipeline to automatically analyze every production core dump from the past year and look for population-level patterns solved crashes that weeks of individual-case inspection could not.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: A transferable debugging methodology—population data over case-by-case reasoning—that ops and SRE teams can adopt for intractable production failures.
insights.quote: building a pipeline to automatically analyze every production core dump from the past year, then looking for population-level patterns

insights.claim: The team had ChatGPT write the script that downloaded core-file prefixes, extracted registers, filtered false positives, and labeled each crash by signature, run in parallel over a year of dumps.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: A real example of using an LLM to build ad-hoc data-analysis tooling for debugging, evidence of AI accelerating internal engineering workflows.
insights.quote: The team had ChatGPT write a script that downloaded a prefix of each core file

insights.claim: What was assumed to be one bug turned out to be two unrelated bugs discovered at the same time, and conflating them made every hypothesis appear contradicted.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: The core lesson for debugging teams: symptoms inconsistent with every hypothesis may signal two mixed phenomena, not an impossible bug.
insights.quote: What they assumed was one bug turned out to be two unrelated bugs, coincidentally discovered at the same time.
insights.locator: What they assumed was one bug

insights.claim: The misaligned-stack crashes traced to a single Azure-region physical host whose CPU silently produced incorrect math results without overheating or throwing machine-check exceptions, and vanished once the host was removed.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: A reminder that silent CPU corruption in cloud fleets is real and undetectable by standard error signals—an operational risk for anyone running at hyperscale.
insights.quote: the CPU was silently producing incorrect results. Not overheating, not throwing machine-check exceptions just quietly getting math wrong.

insights.claim: The root cause was a race in GNU libunwind's _Ux86_64_setcontext that updates the stack pointer before reading the instruction pointer, leaving a one-instruction (~100 picosecond) window where an arriving signal corrupts the instruction pointer, present for 18 years.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Foundational open-source libraries can harbor latent races for nearly two decades, surfacing only at extreme signal frequency—an argument for the value of large-scale users hardening the commons.
insights.quote: The race window is exactly one instruction wide. At modern clock speeds, that's roughly 100 picoseconds.

insights.claim: OpenAI's use of timer_create to deliver a SIGUSR2 signal every few milliseconds of CPU time for per-query accounting generated far more signal events than typical apps, turning a theoretical race into a production crash.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: Shows how workload-specific patterns (high-frequency signals) expose latent bugs invisible to most users—a scale-driven discovery dynamic.
insights.quote: That frequency turned a theoretically possible race into a production crash.

insights.claim: OpenAI upstreamed a fix reordering the instructions so %rip is read before %rsp is updated, plus a self-contained reproducer, and verified libgcc's unwinder lacks the same issue.
insights.speaker: Steef-Jan Wiggers
insights.so_what: A frontier-AI company contributing a fix back to a foundational GNU library benefits the entire C++ ecosystem downstream.
insights.quote: The fix reorders the instructions so that %rip is read before %rsp is updated, eliminating the window entirely.
